content: A single class of customers (sometimes called jobs, packets, calls, or entities depending on domain). Each customer is a discrete, identifiable unit that arrives at the system, waits if the server is busy, is served, and then departs. No attributes beyond identity and arrival time are needed for the canonical model — this minimalism is what makes M/M/1 the foundational simplest case.

One server — the defining feature of the model. The server can handle one customer at a time. The server never fails, takes no breaks, and has exponential service time. In Kendall notation, the '1' in M/M/1 refers to this single server.
One activity — service. A customer arrives, waits if necessary, is served (time drawn from an exponential distribution), and departs. No branching, no routing, no preemption.

Poisson arrivals at rate lambda. Equivalently, inter-arrival times are exponentially distributed with mean 1/lambda. The Poisson process is the memoryless arrival process — knowing how long since the last arrival tells you nothing about when the next one will happen. This is what the first 'M' in M/M/1 denotes.

A single unbounded FIFO queue in front of the server. When the server is busy, arriving customers join the queue and wait in order of arrival. The queue has infinite capacity (the canonical M/M/1 never rejects a customer) and no preemption or priority.
None. There is only one path: arrive -> (possibly wait) -> server -> depart. The absence of routing is part of what makes M/M/1 the simplest non-trivial DES model.

rho = lambda/mu — utilization of the server (must be < 1 for stability). L = rho/(1-rho) — average number of customers in the system. W = 1/(mu-lambda) — average time in system (total sojourn time). L_q = rho^2/(1-rho) — average number of customers waiting (excluding the one in service). W_q = rho/(mu-lambda) — average wait time (queue only, excluding service). Variance of L: rho/(1-rho)^2. Probability of n in system: pi_n = (1-rho) * rho^n (geometric distribution). All of these have closed-form analytical solutions, which is the distinguishing feature of M/M/1 and what makes it useful as a validation benchmark for any simulation tool or custom model.
confidence_note: Unlike most other blueprints where metrics are empirical (tracked by the simulation), M/M/1's metrics have closed-form analytical expressions. A simulation of M/M/1 should reproduce these formulas within statistical tolerance — divergence indicates an implementation error.

content: The canonical model assumes (a) Poisson arrivals independent of queue state (no balking, no reneging), (b) exponential service time (memoryless — how long someone has already been served tells you nothing about how much longer they have), (c) infinite server availability (no breakdowns or breaks), (d) infinite queue capacity (no blocking), (e) FCFS discipline (no priorities, no preemption), (f) steady-state analysis (transient behavior not considered), (g) stability (lambda < mu, otherwise the queue grows without bound). Each of these assumptions can be relaxed to produce a variant model — which is how the queueing literature's taxonomy of models grew.
confidence_note: Each assumption (a)-(g) corresponds to a potential model variant. Relaxing (a) gives balking/reneging variants. Relaxing (d) gives M/M/1/K. Relaxing (b) gives M/G/1. Relaxing (c) gives M/M/1 with breakdowns. Relaxing (e) gives priority-queue variants. This is explicitly how the queueing literature is organized.
